title: Myelodysplastic Syndrome (MDS)
oneLiner: Clonal bone marrow disorder causing ineffective haematopoiesis, cytopenias, and risk of AML; common in older adults.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Neutropenic sepsis**: *single finding*: fever (≥38°C) in a patient with known low neutrophils – start broad-spectrum antibiotics immediately without waiting for cultures.
- **Spontaneous major haemorrhage**: *single finding*: mucosal bleeding (epistaxis, gingival) with platelets <10×10⁹/L – urgent platelet transfusion.
- **Acute leukaemic transformation (AML)**: *single finding*: rapid appearance of blasts in peripheral blood (>20%) with leukostasis symptoms (headache, visual change, dyspnoea) – consider urgent cytoreduction (hydroxycarbamide) and haematology review.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms**: fatigue (anaemia), recurrent infections (neutropenia), easy bruising/bleeding (thrombocytopenia). Often asymptomatic – incidental finding on FBC.
- **Key exam findings**: pallor, petechiae, ecchymoses, hepatosplenomegaly (uncommon), signs of infection (fever, crackles if neutropenic).
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Aplastic anaemia**: pancytopenia, hypocellular marrow. *Splitter*: reticulocyte count low; MDS typically shows hypercellular marrow with dysplasia.
- **Vitamin B12/folate deficiency**: macrocytic anaemia, pancytopenia. *Splitter*: check B12, folate – MDS does not respond to vitamin replacement.
- **Myelofibrosis**: splenomegaly, teardrop cells, JAK2 mutation. *Splitter*: MRI or biopsy shows fibrosis, not dysplasia.
- **Haemolytic anaemia**: reticulocytosis, raised LDH, bilirubin. *Splitter*: positive direct antiglobulin test; peripheral film no dysplasia.
- **PNH**: cytopenias with thrombosis, dark urine. *Splitter*: flow cytometry for CD55/59.
- **Secondary causes**: drugs (chemotherapy), toxins, HIV – check history.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside**: observations (including temperature). If febrile, septic screen: blood cultures, urine, CXR.
- **Bloods**: FBC + differential (anaemia, MCV), reticulocyte count, peripheral blood film (dysplastic neutrophils, ring sideroblasts, blasts). Iron studies + B12 + folate + red cell folate. LDH, haptoglobin (exclude haemolysis). U&Es, LFTs. Viral serology (HIV, hepatitis, EBV, CMV).
- **Imaging**: CXR if infection suspected. Abdomen US if splenomegaly.
- **Special tests**: Bone marrow aspirate + trephine biopsy with cytogenetics (FISH for del5q, -7, complex) and molecular panel (SF3B1, TP53). Iron stain for ring sideroblasts.
- **Diagnostic criteria**: WHO classification (2016/2022) – blasts <5% marrow for MDS (5-19% MDS-EB, ≥20% AML). Dysplasia in ≥10% of one or more lineages. Cytopenias: Hb <100, neutrophils <1.8, platelets <100. IPSS-R for risk stratification.
- **Never-miss test**: **Bone marrow aspirate + trephine** with cytogenetics – omission means no definitive diagnosis or risk stratification.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED)**: If neutropenic sepsis – start empiric antibiotics per local protocol (e.g., piperacillin-tazobactam). If thrombocytopenic bleeding – platelet transfusion if platelets <10 (stable) or <20-50 (active bleed). If anaemic symptomatic – RBC transfusion (Hb <70 or symptomatic). If hyperleukocytosis (WCC >100) with leukostasis – consider hydroxycarbamide (1-2g PO stat, [check local policy]) and consult haematology. Manage complication first.
- **Ward (first 24-72h)**: Complete diagnostic bone marrow. Supportive care: RBC transfusion threshold Hb <80 (individualise); platelet transfusion for procedures/bleeding. G-CSF only for severe neutropenia with infection (not routine). Iron chelation if transfusion-dependent and ferritin high – discuss haematology. Allopurinol if high tumour lysis risk (only with high blast count). Educate on neutropenic precautions.
- **Long-term / outpatient**: Risk stratify using IPSS-R. Lower-risk: ESAs if low EPO; lenalidomide for del5q; luspatercept for ring sideroblasts. Higher-risk: hypomethylating agents (azacitidine/decitabine) or allogeneic SCT (fit patients). Supportive transfusions as needed.
- **Default Orders (if admitted)**: IV fluids: 0.9% saline 125 ml/hr if febrile or for drug admin (otherwise oral). VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily (caution if platelets <50). Diet: low-microbial if neutropenic (avoid raw/undercooked). Observations: NEWS2 4-6 hourly. FBC daily.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents**: **Azacitidine** (higher-risk MDS): 75 mg/m² SC daily x7 days per 28-day cycle (specialist). **Lenalidomide** (del5q): 10 mg PO daily days 1-21 per 28-day cycle. **Erythropoietin alfa**: 40 000 units SC weekly (initial; [check local policy]). **Luspatercept**: 1 mg/kg SC every 3 weeks (specialist). **Hydroxycarbamide** (cytoreduction): 500 mg-2 g PO daily (titrate). **Filgrastim** (G-CSF): 300 mcg SC daily up to 5 days for severe neutropenia with infection. **RBC transfusion**: 1-2 units over 4 hours. **Platelets**: 1 adult therapeutic dose.
- **Common alternatives**: Decitabine, deferasirox (iron chelation), allopurinol 300 mg PO daily.
- **Key contraindications / interactions**: Lenalidomide: pregnancy (strict contraception). Azacitidine: severe hepatic impairment. Hydroxycarbamide: teratogenic. Filgrastim: avoid in rapidly progressive CML. Erythropoietin: caution if hypertension/ thrombosis.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment**: Lenalidomide: reduce dose if CrCl <60 (e.g., 5 mg daily). Azacitidine: avoid if severe renal. Hydroxycarbamide: adjust if CrCl <30.
- **Most dangerous dose error**: **Lenalidomide – prescribing without negative pregnancy test and contraception in women of childbearing potential (teratogenic).** Also, **azacitidine – accidental intrathecal administration (fatal) – ensure SC/IV only.**
sections.Complications:
- **Acute**: Neutropenic sepsis, bleeding, transfusion reactions, tumour lysis (high blast count), drug reactions (lenalidomide rash), thrombosis (ESA).
- **Chronic**: Transfusion iron overload (heart, liver, endocrine), progression to AML, chronic infections/bleeding.
sections.Prognosis: - IPSS-R risk groups: very low median ~8.8 years, low ~5.3 years, intermediate ~3 years, high ~1.6 years, very high ~0.8 years. Higher-risk groups have greater AML transformation. Supportive care improves quality of life.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always check peripheral blood film** in elderly with unexplained anaemia – it may show dysplastic features and guide urgent bone marrow.
- **Repeat FBC in 4-6 weeks** if borderline cytopenias – MDS can be initially stable; don't overdiagnose but don't miss progression.
- **Proactively discuss transfusion goals** with patient – many older patients tolerate lower Hb, but may need frequent transfusions.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not give G-CSF routinely for all neutropenic patients** – only use with infection and ANC <0.5; overuse may increase transformation risk.
- **Do not assume pancytopenia = MDS without bone marrow** – rule out drugs, infection, B12 deficiency.
- **Do not delay bone marrow biopsy for risk stratification** – waiting for counts to improve is wrong; diagnosis requires marrow.

rawMarkdown:
## Myelodysplastic Syndrome (MDS)
**One-liner:** Clonal bone marrow disorder causing ineffective haematopoiesis, cytopenias, and risk of AML; common in older adults.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Neutropenic sepsis**: *single finding*: fever (≥38°C) in a patient with known low neutrophils – start broad-spectrum antibiotics immediately without waiting for cultures.
- **Spontaneous major haemorrhage**: *single finding*: mucosal bleeding (epistaxis, gingival) with platelets <10×10⁹/L – urgent platelet transfusion.
- **Acute leukaemic transformation (AML)**: *single finding*: rapid appearance of blasts in peripheral blood (>20%) with leukostasis symptoms (headache, visual change, dyspnoea) – consider urgent cytoreduction (hydroxycarbamide) and haematology review.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms**: fatigue (anaemia), recurrent infections (neutropenia), easy bruising/bleeding (thrombocytopenia). Often asymptomatic – incidental finding on FBC.
- **Key exam findings**: pallor, petechiae, ecchymoses, hepatosplenomegaly (uncommon), signs of infection (fever, crackles if neutropenic).

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Aplastic anaemia**: pancytopenia, hypocellular marrow. *Splitter*: reticulocyte count low; MDS typically shows hypercellular marrow with dysplasia.
- **Vitamin B12/folate deficiency**: macrocytic anaemia, pancytopenia. *Splitter*: check B12, folate – MDS does not respond to vitamin replacement.
- **Myelofibrosis**: splenomegaly, teardrop cells, JAK2 mutation. *Splitter*: MRI or biopsy shows fibrosis, not dysplasia.
- **Haemolytic anaemia**: reticulocytosis, raised LDH, bilirubin. *Splitter*: positive direct antiglobulin test; peripheral film no dysplasia.
- **PNH**: cytopenias with thrombosis, dark urine. *Splitter*: flow cytometry for CD55/59.
- **Secondary causes**: drugs (chemotherapy), toxins, HIV – check history.

### Investigations
- **Bedside**: observations (including temperature). If febrile, septic screen: blood cultures, urine, CXR.
- **Bloods**: FBC + differential (anaemia, MCV), reticulocyte count, peripheral blood film (dysplastic neutrophils, ring sideroblasts, blasts). Iron studies + B12 + folate + red cell folate. LDH, haptoglobin (exclude haemolysis). U&Es, LFTs. Viral serology (HIV, hepatitis, EBV, CMV).
- **Imaging**: CXR if infection suspected. Abdomen US if splenomegaly.
- **Special tests**: Bone marrow aspirate + trephine biopsy with cytogenetics (FISH for del5q, -7, complex) and molecular panel (SF3B1, TP53). Iron stain for ring sideroblasts.
- **Diagnostic criteria**: WHO classification (2016/2022) – blasts <5% marrow for MDS (5-19% MDS-EB, ≥20% AML). Dysplasia in ≥10% of one or more lineages. Cytopenias: Hb <100, neutrophils <1.8, platelets <100. IPSS-R for risk stratification.
- **Never-miss test**: **Bone marrow aspirate + trephine** with cytogenetics – omission means no definitive diagnosis or risk stratification.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED)**: If neutropenic sepsis – start empiric antibiotics per local protocol (e.g., piperacillin-tazobactam). If thrombocytopenic bleeding – platelet transfusion if platelets <10 (stable) or <20-50 (active bleed). If anaemic symptomatic – RBC transfusion (Hb <70 or symptomatic). If hyperleukocytosis (WCC >100) with leukostasis – consider hydroxycarbamide (1-2g PO stat, [check local policy]) and consult haematology. Manage complication first.
- **Ward (first 24-72h)**: Complete diagnostic bone marrow. Supportive care: RBC transfusion threshold Hb <80 (individualise); platelet transfusion for procedures/bleeding. G-CSF only for severe neutropenia with infection (not routine). Iron chelation if transfusion-dependent and ferritin high – discuss haematology. Allopurinol if high tumour lysis risk (only with high blast count). Educate on neutropenic precautions.
- **Long-term / outpatient**: Risk stratify using IPSS-R. Lower-risk: ESAs if low EPO; lenalidomide for del5q; luspatercept for ring sideroblasts. Higher-risk: hypomethylating agents (azacitidine/decitabine) or allogeneic SCT (fit patients). Supportive transfusions as needed.
- **Default Orders (if admitted)**: IV fluids: 0.9% saline 125 ml/hr if febrile or for drug admin (otherwise oral). VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily (caution if platelets <50). Diet: low-microbial if neutropenic (avoid raw/undercooked). Observations: NEWS2 4-6 hourly. FBC daily.

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents**: **Azacitidine** (higher-risk MDS): 75 mg/m² SC daily x7 days per 28-day cycle (specialist). **Lenalidomide** (del5q): 10 mg PO daily days 1-21 per 28-day cycle. **Erythropoietin alfa**: 40 000 units SC weekly (initial; [check local policy]). **Luspatercept**: 1 mg/kg SC every 3 weeks (specialist). **Hydroxycarbamide** (cytoreduction): 500 mg-2 g PO daily (titrate). **Filgrastim** (G-CSF): 300 mcg SC daily up to 5 days for severe neutropenia with infection. **RBC transfusion**: 1-2 units over 4 hours. **Platelets**: 1 adult therapeutic dose.
- **Common alternatives**: Decitabine, deferasirox (iron chelation), allopurinol 300 mg PO daily.
- **Key contraindications / interactions**: Lenalidomide: pregnancy (strict contraception). Azacitidine: severe hepatic impairment. Hydroxycarbamide: teratogenic. Filgrastim: avoid in rapidly progressive CML. Erythropoietin: caution if hypertension/ thrombosis.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment**: Lenalidomide: reduce dose if CrCl <60 (e.g., 5 mg daily). Azacitidine: avoid if severe renal. Hydroxycarbamide: adjust if CrCl <30.
- **Most dangerous dose error**: **Lenalidomide – prescribing without negative pregnancy test and contraception in women of childbearing potential (teratogenic).** Also, **azacitidine – accidental intrathecal administration (fatal) – ensure SC/IV only.**

### Complications
- **Acute**: Neutropenic sepsis, bleeding, transfusion reactions, tumour lysis (high blast count), drug reactions (lenalidomide rash), thrombosis (ESA).
- **Chronic**: Transfusion iron overload (heart, liver, endocrine), progression to AML, chronic infections/bleeding.

### Prognosis
- IPSS-R risk groups: very low median ~8.8 years, low ~5.3 years, intermediate ~3 years, high ~1.6 years, very high ~0.8 years. Higher-risk groups have greater AML transformation. Supportive care improves quality of life.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always check peripheral blood film** in elderly with unexplained anaemia – it may show dysplastic features and guide urgent bone marrow.
- **Repeat FBC in 4-6 weeks** if borderline cytopenias – MDS can be initially stable; don't overdiagnose but don't miss progression.
- **Proactively discuss transfusion goals** with patient – many older patients tolerate lower Hb, but may need frequent transfusions.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not give G-CSF routinely for all neutropenic patients** – only use with infection and ANC <0.5; overuse may increase transformation risk.
- **Do not assume pancytopenia = MDS without bone marrow** – rule out drugs, infection, B12 deficiency.
- **Do not delay bone marrow biopsy for risk stratification** – waiting for counts to improve is wrong; diagnosis requires marrow.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** Fever >
